paragraphs.body: Have you ever looked into someone’s eyes and there was nothing but darkness behind them? Seeing this darkness can only make us wonder what they went through to have such a lifeless expression in their eyes. This darkness is one that is prevalent in the eyes of war veterans. The first time I ever noticed this look was when I met my new platoon sergeant in my first year in the military. It was a terrifying sight to look into his eyes and see nothing but an abyss. On the outside, you never could have been able to tell he had been through something by the way he joked around and spoke. His eyes told a different and much darker story. My understanding of this expression has been shaped through my journey thus far. Our languages, cultures, and experiences all shape the lives that we live.

annotations.text: The first time I ever noticed this look was when I met my new platoon sergeant in my first year in the military. My understanding of this expression has been shaped through my journey thus far. Our languages, cultures, and experiences all shape the lives that we live.
annotations.comment: Yes. Great addition! This concrete and specific description sets up the important ideas for the essay and creates an excellent sense of authenticity and credibility. Nice job.

paragraphs.body: I would consider my home language to be the language I speak with my brother. I grew up with my brother and my sister. I was the youngest, my sister 2 years older than me, and my brother 2 years older than her. Growing up, I was always close with both of my siblings, but in very different ways. With my sister, it was typically just hanging out and talking or her helping me with schoolwork. I would see her as a comforting presence with no judgements. My relationship with my brother was the polar opposite. There was little to no comfort around him and everything I said or did mattered. Perfection was expected in every aspect of life between my brother and I. When we would play catch outside and he made a bad throw that I was unable to get I was told, “It’s your fault” and “You should’ve caught that.” If I tossed a water bottle to him and he went to catch it, but the bottle wasn’t perfectly rotated to make it the easiest to catch, it was a “terrible throw” and he “wasn’t surprised” because I suck at everything I do. This was my home language and there was no room for error. Most people would be torn apart by receiving no positive encouragement and you may start to see the light in their eyes dim; however, this is a language I adopted from my brother at a young age and we have continued to use it ever since. Instead of letting it eat at me and get down on myself, I fed off of it and considered it to be an environment that fostered tough love. I turned this language into a driving factor that would stay with me throughout my entire life thus far. With degrading language being a part of my life growing up, what better route to take than the military where this type of language was not only common, but preferred.

My secret language is militarese. It is a language rooted with derogatory terms meant to dehumanize you and essentially turn you into a robot in order to carry out your mission with no hesitation, which is the first lesson you learn in boot camp. As soon as you get off the bus and place your feet on the yellow footprints, the drill instructors inform you of your new personality. The words, “I,” “Me,” “Mine,” and any other word used to refer to yourself are now banned. Instead, you fill these words with the phrase, “This recruit.” The reason for doing this is to remove any thoughts of individualism you may have and if you are ever caught saying any of those words you would be met with immediate punishment. Along with this, for the entire duration of boot camp you are spoken to as if you are lower than dirt. This same type of treatment would follow you for about the first year and a half to two years of your career until you complete your first deployment. As for me, I had been treated this way for the previous 18 years of my life up to that point, which made this type of language almost comforting to me. I would watch the faces of my peers dripping with anxiety thinking that they were not good enough while I would stand there staring my seniors directly in the eyes with nothing but the same mindset of my childhood. I will prove you wrong. I will be so perfect that you won’t ever be able to doubt me. After these verbal beratements, I would see all of my peers looking defeated and beating themselves up over it and I would think to myself how weak they were. If they can’t even handle being scolded how would they ever handle the effects of war? I looked down on many of my peers during my first year and always felt that I was tougher and better than them. For every peer group in the Marine Corps there were always at least two or three individuals in each who were known for not being able to handle the pressures that came with the job. They would often be treated as lesser than the rest of their peer group. They were like ghosts. It was as if you looked into their eyes and there was nothing but darkness behind them. My seniors would always tell us to not worry about them and that they weren’t really part of the unit. Hearing this I acted accordingly around them, not paying them any mind when they were around. I looked at them the same I would look at my peers who were struggling, like they were weak individuals who were just wasting everyone’s time and energy. I held this view my entire boot year and into my first deployment.

It wasn’t until my first deployment that I realized something that was very crucial going forward. I can still remember a weekend brief we were given about a month into the deployment. It was all very typical stuff they would repeat before we were cut loose for the weekend, “Don’t drink and drive, don’t knock up any girls, don’t fight any locals outside of the bars, if you’re gonna fight a local make sure you at least win, and don’t go anywhere without a buddy.” At the end of the typical routine, an extra point was added in, “We were informed that two Marines from our unit had jumped out of their windows in the barracks last night killing themselves.” He proceeded to talk about resources if you were feeling down and wrapped up the briefing. The tragic news was delivered no differently than the rest of the monotonous safety brief information. We would receive similar briefs what felt like once per month of more Marines committing suicide. It was almost always a junior Marine who up to this point has been nothing but degraded and told that he means nothing and he’s worthless. It wasn’t until this briefing that I realized the impact of the way we were being treated and talked to had on other people. The Marines I mentioned prior who were seen as outcasts and not really with the unit were on suicide watch. The look of darkness in their eyes was a loss of the will to live. It took the lives of nine Marines on my first deployment for me to finally see through the façade that my senior Marines would feed me. After that briefing, I made a promise to myself that when I am in charge of junior Marines that I will never make them feel as if their life is not worth living. I learned to lead and teach my Marines with a balance of tough love that I was taught growing up with the right touch of empathy for them to know that I am being hard on them for a good reason. I would always give them the “why” behind the training they were doing or the reason why they were being punished. I made sure they didn’t just fear me, but that they knew that if they ever had any issues with anything in their life, work related or personal issues, I would always be there to help them. This line between hard training and humane treatment is very thin or possibly not even there at all. Perhaps there is no intersection point between these methods. Regardless, every attempt should be made to discover this balance. I hope that my junior Marines were able continue the search for this new method as I once embarked on as well. Hopefully they can keep taking small steps in the right direction in order to achieve this seemingly impossible balance.

annotations.text: The look of darkness in their eyes was a loss of the will to live.
Regardless, every attempt should be made to discover this balance.

paragraphs.body: Throughout my journey, I have witnessed this darkness in the eyes of many of my fellow Marines which stemmed from things other than actual war. At first, I believed only going through war would give you that look since my first experience seeing it was with a war veteran. I quickly learned that this is only one of the ways it can happen. Instead of being at war, these individuals were experiencing war within themselves. After becoming familiar with recognizing this darkness in those around me, I made it a goal of mine to do what I could to keep the glimmering light of life alive in their eyes.

annotations.text: Throughout my journey, I have witnessed this darkness in the eyes of many of my fellow Marines which stemmed from things other than actual war.

paragraphs.body: Turn your Marines into emotionless robots, but make sure they are feeling happy. Train them rigorously and scream at them until they hate your guts, but create a sense of brotherhood amongst your men. Treat your boots as if they are useless sacks of meat and cannot do anything right, but ensure morale is high among the lower enlisted. Make your boots first year more painful and scarring than hell, but act confused when they give up on life.

annotations.comment: Yes! Great poem. This speaks directly to the ideas at the heart of this essay. Nice details and voice, too. Well done.
annotations.context_right: Treat your boots as if they are useless sacks of meat and cannot do anything right,
